critic_raw:
The design uses a strong temporal split with patient-level integrity — this is appropriate. However, structured data preprocessing is explicitly stated as fit on the entire dataset before splitting ('All preprocessing transformers are designed for stability in parameter estimation by being fit on the entire data prior to splitting'). This means normalization statistics from the test period (months 49-60) influence the feature scaling applied during training and validation. For heart failure patients where lab values (BNP, creatinine) and vitals may shift seasonally or due to treatment protocol changes, this leakage is non-trivial.

The critical design flaw is the baseline choice. The baseline is a unimodal model using clinical notes only (BioBERT + BiLSTM). The multi-modal model adds both echocardiogram images AND structured data (ejection fraction, BNP, comorbidities, medications, LOS). Ejection fraction and BNP are among the strongest known predictors of heart failure readmission in the clinical literature. The multi-modal model vs. notes-only baseline compares a model with highly predictive structured cardiology features against one without them. Any performance gain cannot be attributed to imaging adding value; it could be entirely from structured HF-specific features. The stated hypothesis tests whether 'multi-modal (notes + imaging)' beats 'notes alone' but the experiment is actually testing 'notes + imaging + structured HF labs vs. notes alone.'

Echocardiogram images are processed through DenseNet-121 pretrained on ImageNet with frozen weights. ImageNet features represent natural image textures and objects; echocardiogram images are ultrasound sequences with fundamentally different statistical properties (speckle noise, temporal motion, grayscale dynamic range). Frozen ImageNet features applied to echocardiograms may produce largely noise-level representations. If the imaging branch contributes near-zero signal, the experiment cannot distinguish 'imaging helps' from 'imaging branch is noise but doesn't hurt.'

The hypothesis is declared supported only if multi-modal achieves higher AUROC than baseline 'consistently across all subgroups.' With six subgroup analyses and no multiple comparison correction, the probability of finding at least one inconsistent subgroup by chance is substantial — this consistency criterion is more likely to produce false rejections of a true hypothesis than it is to be a meaningful safeguard.
all_issues_raised: Structured data preprocessing (z-score normalization) is fit on the entire dataset before temporal splitting — normalization statistics from the test period (months 49-60) leak into training feature scaling.

The baseline is clinical notes-only; the multi-modal model adds both images AND structured HF features (ejection fraction, BNP) that are among the strongest known predictors of HF readmission — any observed gain cannot be attributed to imaging and cannot test the stated hypothesis about multi-modal integration.

DenseNet-121 is frozen with ImageNet weights for echocardiogram ultrasound images — ImageNet pretraining is domain-inappropriate for cardiac ultrasound (speckle noise, grayscale, temporal), and frozen features from this domain mismatch may contribute near-zero imaging signal.

The hypothesis confirmation criterion requires multi-modal superiority 'consistently across all subgroups' without multiple comparison correction — with six subgroup analyses, this criterion mechanically inflates false rejection of a genuine multi-modal advantage.

AUROC is justified as primary metric over AUPRC despite a presumably high (~25-30%) readmission rate in heart failure patients where AUPRC would better distinguish model performance in the actionable high-risk region.
all_issues_adjudicated: Structured data preprocessing (z-score normalization) is fit on the entire dataset before temporal splitting — normalization statistics from the test period (months 49-60) leak into training feature scaling.

The baseline is clinical notes-only; the multi-modal model adds both images AND structured HF features (ejection fraction, BNP) that are among the strongest known predictors of HF readmission — any observed gain cannot be attributed to imaging and cannot test the stated hypothesis about multi-modal integration.

DenseNet-121 is frozen with ImageNet weights for echocardiogram ultrasound images — ImageNet pretraining is domain-inappropriate for cardiac ultrasound (speckle noise, grayscale, temporal), and frozen features from this domain mismatch may contribute near-zero imaging signal.

The hypothesis confirmation criterion requires multi-modal superiority 'consistently across all subgroups' without multiple comparison correction — with six subgroup analyses, this criterion mechanically inflates false rejection of a genuine multi-modal advantage.

AUROC is justified as primary metric over AUPRC despite a presumably high (~25-30%) readmission rate in heart failure patients where AUPRC would better distinguish model performance in the actionable high-risk region.
